preamble: The shift from a retroflex stop [ʈ] (or its voiced counterpart [ɖ]) to a rhotic sound is a classic form of lenition. In intervocalic positions, the complete closure of the stop is often replaced by a brief tap or flap [ɽ], which is acoustically and articulatory identical to a rhotic consonant in many languages.

examples.note: Historical voiced retroflex stops regularly weakened into retroflex flaps in modern North Indian languages.
Synchronic lenition where the retroflex stop is realized as a flap or approximant in rapid speech.
